title: SEGMENT 12 — Towana Looney: the longest survival so far

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Towana Looney: the longest survival so far

Towana Looney is a woman from Alabama who, as of the most recent updates I have, is alive with a pig kidney inside her — and has been alive with it longer than any other patient in the history of clinical xenotransplantation.
Her story is the one that, more than the others, is telling the field that this might actually work.
Looney is 53.
She donated a kidney to her mother, who was in renal failure, back in 1999 — twenty-five years before her own transplant.
Some years after that donation, Looney herself developed pregnancy-related complications that destroyed her remaining kidney.
She had been on dialysis for eight years.

Like Slayman, she was highly sensitized — by virtue of pregnancies and dialysis-related transfusions, her body had developed broad antibodies against human tissues, making her a very poor candidate for a human kidney transplant.
She was on the list, but the chance of finding a compatible donor was small.
She was running out of time.
In November 2024, at NYU Langone — the same team led by Robert Montgomery that had transplanted Pisano — Looney received an eGenesis pig kidney.
Same 69-edit pig design as Slayman and Pisano.
Same surgical approach.
The transplant went smoothly.
She produced urine immediately.
She recovered in a way that, by the team's account, was the smoothest of any xeno patient to date.
She went home.
She gave interviews.
She said she felt better than she had in years.
She came off dialysis.
Her creatinine stabilized in the near-normal range.
She walked around Times Square as a tourist a few weeks after surgery, just for the experience of feeling well enough to do it.

As of the time I'm preparing these notes — and I want to be careful because this is the fastest-moving part of the lecture, and by the time you hear it the situation may have evolved — Towana Looney has lived longer with a pig kidney than any other patient.
The most recent public updates have her past four months, into 2025, with continuing kidney function.
The team is reporting periodic episodes of rejection that have been managed with adjustments to her immunosuppression.
The kidney is, so far, holding.
If you want one mental image for the 2024 kidney wave, here it is.
Slayman, Pisano, Looney — three patients in a row, each living longer with their pig kidney than the last.
The trajectory is the right direction.
The first patient functioned for seven weeks, the second for several weeks, the third for months and counting.
The team is learning with each case how to manage the immune protocol, the supportive care, the patient selection, the surveillance.
The bar is moving.
That said, I want to be honest.
Three patients is not a clinical trial.
The fact that Looney is doing well is enormously encouraging, but extrapolating from one patient to a treatment for tens of thousands of dialysis patients is not warranted.
The patients we are seeing now are no-options patients, transplanted under compassionate use authorizations, with the most experienced teams in the world watching them around the clock.
The transition to a formal clinical trial — and from a trial to a routine standard of care — is a long process.
We are at the beginning of it.
The field's leadership knows this.
There is a planned formal clinical trial for pig kidneys, expected to begin in 2025, that will enroll a defined cohort of patients under a more conventional protocol.
The compassionate use cases — Bennett, Faucette, Slayman, Pisano, Looney — are the bridge from preclinical baboon work to that formal trial.
They tell us, broadly: it can work, the immunology is manageable, the surveillance must be careful, the patients matter.
The formal trial is where we will learn whether xeno can become medicine.
